did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) to classify dermatoscopic lesion images into cancerous and non-cancerous categories (binary classification) and into nine specific types of skin cancer (multi-class classification). The study achieved an accuracy of 92% for the binary classifier and 97% for the multi-class classifier using the ISIC 2019 dataset.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and implementation of a Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for binary and multi-class classification of skin cancer using the ISIC 2019 dataset. It reports specific performance metrics (e.g., 92.08% accuracy for binary classification, 97.53% for multi-class) and includes a 'Materials and Methods' section detailing the dataset preparation and model architecture, as well as a 'Results' section with confusion matrices and comparison tables. Although published in a conference proceedings (CONIT 2023), it contains original results rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Image-based Classification of Skin Cancer using Convolution Neural Network
brief_summary: This study proposes a web-based application utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks (CNN) for the binary and multi-class classification of skin cancer using dermatoscopic images from the ISIC 2019 dataset. The binary classifier distinguishes between melanoma and non-melanoma cases, achieving an accuracy of 92.08%. The multi-class model categorizes lesions into nine specific types, including healthy skin, and achieves an accuracy of 97.53%. Image preprocessing techniques, such as morphological black hat transformation and inpainting, were employed to remove noise and hair artifacts. The results demonstrate that the proposed CNN models provide an effective tool for the preliminary diagnosis and early detection of skin cancer.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification (Melanoma vs. Non-Melanoma/Normal) and Multi-class classification (9 specific skin cancer types including Healthy Skin)
all_task_types.details: The paper implements a CNN for binary classification (distinguishing cancerous from non-cancerous) with 92.08% accuracy and a multi-class classification model categorizing images into 9 classes (Actinic Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Benign Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Healthy Skin, Melanocytic Nevus, Melanoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Vascular Lesion) with 97.53% accuracy. The output includes class labels and likelihoods (probabilities) for the classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A collection of skin lesion images obtained from the official ISIC challenge website, containing images for eight types of skin cancers.

use.training: Used for training the binary and multi-class CNN models. The dataset was divided into folders according to lesion type labels. For the multi-class model, data augmentation (flipping and rotating) was performed, and the dataset was converted to 1000 balanced images to avoid overfitting. For binary classification, 10,982 images were used for training.
use.testing: Used for testing. 3,562 unlabeled images were used for the binary classification evaluation. The multi-class model was evaluated on a testing set (specific count not explicitly separated from the total in the text, but derived from the augmented/balanced set).

datasets_used.name: SIIM-ISIC Melanoma Classification Dataset
datasets_used.description: A dataset providing healthy/benign skin images, obtained from the official ISIC challenge website.

use.training: 1000 images of healthy/benign skin were added to the ISIC 2019 dataset to form the ninth class for the multi-class classification task.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No hypothesis testing was reported in the paper.

ISIC 2019 Dataset (Binary Classification.notes: The model achieved higher sensitivity and specificity compared to existing works [3], [4], [10]. Accuracy is similar to existing systems [3], [7], [10].

ISIC 2019 + SIIM-ISIC Dataset (Multi-cla.dataset_preparation: Dataset of 25,000 images was imbalanced; data augmentation (flipping and rotating) was performed to create a balanced dataset of 1,000 images to avoid overfitting.
ISIC 2019 + SIIM-ISIC Dataset (Multi-cla.notes: The model detected Vascular lesion with the highest accuracy (99.78%). The proposed system achieved satisfactory results for multi-class classification of nine classes, whereas existing work was limited to a maximum of five classes.

binary_classifier.structure: 4 pairs of convolutional and pooling layers, followed by a flattening layer and two dense layers
binary_classifier.regularization: l2 regularizer on the last dense layer

multi_class_classifier.structure: 6 pairs of convolutional and pooling layers, followed by a flattening layer and two dense layers

classification_or_output_head.binary: Linear activation (behaves similar to Linear SVM)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Neural networks connected on two levels of classification
preprocessing_techniques: Morphological black hat transformation for noise and feature enhancement
Inpainting algorithm for hair and artifact removal
Data augmentation via flipping and rotating images
Dataset balancing by converting imbalanced data to 1000 balanced images

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to images to increase dataset size and balance the training dataset for the multi-class classification model.
Applied to images to increase dataset size and balance the training dataset for the multi-class classification model.
The imbalanced dataset of 25,000 images was converted into a balanced dataset of 1,000 images using flipping and rotating to avoid overfitting.

conclusions: The proposed CNN-based binary classifier achieved an accuracy of 92.08%, with sensitivity (92.25%) and specificity (87.92%) higher than existing works, while accuracy was similar to prior systems.
The CNN-based multi-class classifier achieved an average accuracy of 97.53% across nine classes, outperforming existing multi-class studies which were limited to a maximum of five classes.
The multi-class model detected Vascular Lesion with the highest accuracy (99.78%) and reported an average specificity of 98.61%, which was better than existing work.
The proposed system serves as an effective tool for the preliminary diagnosis and early detection of skin cancer, aiding in the combat of its spread and lethality.
Data augmentation (flipping and rotating) was necessary to balance the imbalanced ISIC 2019 dataset (converted to 1,000 balanced images) to avoid overfitting in the multi-class model.
The study demonstrates that CNN models provide significant improvements in accuracy, precision, and recall compared to other existing models for skin cancer classification.
important_remarks: The multi-class classification model was trained on a severely reduced dataset of only 1,000 balanced images, derived from an original imbalanced set of 25,000 images via augmentation (flipping and rotating), which raises concerns about data leakage and generalizability.
The study lacks external validation, relying solely on the ISIC 2019 and SIIM-ISIC datasets without testing on independent, out-of-distribution clinical data.
The binary classifier achieved 92.08% accuracy, which the authors note is 'similar to existing works,' suggesting the performance improvement over state-of-the-art may be marginal.
The multi-class model reported an unusually high average specificity of 98.61% and accuracy of 97.53% on a small, artificially balanced test set, which may indicate overfitting or optimistic bias.
The paper does not report confidence intervals or statistical significance tests for the performance metrics, limiting the robustness of the comparative claims.

The proposed system is presented as a 'preliminary diagnostic tool' for early detection, but the lack of clinical trial data or comparison with dermatologist performance limits its immediate clinical relevance.

Data preprocessing included morphological black hat transformation and inpainting for hair removal, but the impact of these techniques on feature integrity and model robustness is not quantitatively analyzed.
